posts.summary: Hannes Mühleisen introduces DuckDB at FOSDEM 2020 as an embeddable analytical database designed to bring proper data management to the messy world of data analytics. He argues that the current state of data analysis—CSV files, crude query engines like Pandas, and hand-rolled binary formats—represents solved problems being poorly reinvented. DuckDB follows the SQLite model of being a zero-dependency, single-file, in-process database library, but optimized for analytical workloads rather than transactions. The core technical innovation is vectorized processing, which operates on chunks of data that fit in CPU cache, achieving roughly 40x speedups over traditional row-at-a-time engines. He emphasizes rigorous quality assurance including millions of test queries, stolen test cases from other databases, and continuous benchmarking.
key_points: Data analytics tooling is a mess of CSV files, crude query engines, and hand-rolled binary formats—all solving problems that databases solved 50 years ago
DuckDB follows SQLite's model (zero dependencies, single file, in-process) but targets analytical workloads instead of transactions
Vectorized processing operates on chunks that fit in CPU cache, achieving ~40x speedup over row-at-a-time engines and handling data larger than memory
Quality assurance includes millions of SQL test queries, stolen test cases from other databases including scraping SQL Server's website, and query fuzzing
DuckDB deliberately avoids holding data hostage—fast data transfer to host languages means users aren't punished for pulling data out
money_quotes: It is common to start these kind of talks with a description of how terrible the state of this world is. This is no exception.
Once people decide that CSV files are too slow, they start inventing their own crude hand-rolled binary formats that are on disk, maybe, and start processing those.
These things are solved problems. We have data management systems that have been around for 50 years or so.
We have zero external dependencies. This is really something that took a lot of work, but it is something that we believe is actually quite necessary for a library to be successful.
One of the limitations of things like Pandas is that once your data becomes bigger than memory, you're screwed.
We went around and steal everyone's test cases. So with SQL engines you can do this because they all have the same sort of query language.
We don't punish you for pulling a large chunk out of a system. We don't hold the data hostage.
posts.key_insight: Data analytics suffers from constant reinvention of solved database problems, and DuckDB applies the SQLite embedding model with vectorized analytical processing to bring proper data management into analysts' existing workflows.

posts.summary: Hannes Mühleisen explains DuckDB's design as an in-process analytical SQL engine, contrasting it with client-server architectures like Spark and traditional data warehouses. Born from conversations with statisticians who needed serious data processing without infrastructure complexity, DuckDB uses vectorized query processing to deliver high-performance OLAP in an embeddable library. He describes the SQLite-like deployment model applied to analytics, zero-copy integration with Python/R/Arrow ecosystems, and the WebAssembly version running entirely in the browser. The conversation covers DuckDB's single-file storage with checksummed blocks, the open governance model through the DuckDB Foundation, and the project's focus on out-of-core execution to complete queries regardless of memory constraints.
key_points: DuckDB fills a gap as the first in-process OLAP engine, enabling analytical SQL wherever the application runs without server setup or data movement
A single DuckDB node matched or beat a 32-node Spark cluster on taxi benchmark queries, exposing massive overuse of distributed infrastructure for small-to-medium data
Zero-copy integration with pandas, R data frames, and Arrow allows DuckDB to query existing in-memory structures without materialization, creating novel memory management challenges
The vectorized query processing design was chosen over JIT compilation to keep the binary small (~10MB) and the codebase approachable for contributors
DuckDB's governance separates the non-profit DuckDB Foundation (holds IP, MIT license, cannot be changed) from DuckDB Labs (commercial support), protecting the project from corporate interests
money_quotes: If you're going to be installed on everyone's laptop out there, you're going to have thousands of broken machines that you're running on.
Too often big data is just the same fairly small data copied a million times because people have no other choice than to replicate everything.
It's as if you drive a dump truck around as your daily driver. While Spark has its place, I also think it's complete overkill in like 99% of the use cases it's used for.
We are kind of data communists in that sense that we treat all data sources equally.
Your assumption of how they should hold it is wrong. So you have to basically make the handle like pink so they kind of see it and hold it correctly.
I think there is no good reason to have these mini clusters around because you're getting all the pain of running clusters and basically all the compute is eaten up by the coordination.

The most unexpected thing is that people cared about this thing in the first place.
posts.tone: conversational, opinionated, technically grounded, self-deprecating humor
posts.key_insight: Moving the analytical database engine into the application process eliminates infrastructure complexity and unlocks orders of magnitude of untapped single-node performance that most organizations waste on distributed systems they don't need.

The project is governed by a non-profit foundation with MIT licensing, while DuckDB Labs provides commercial support.

posts.summary: Hannes Mühleisen explains DuckDB's design as the first in-process OLAP database, inspired by SQLite's embedded architecture but built for analytical workloads. He describes how DuckDB's vectorized execution engine processes data in chunks of 1,024 values to amortize interpretation overhead while keeping intermediates in CPU cache. He argues that single-node performance has been badly neglected in favor of distributed systems, demonstrating that DuckDB on a laptop can outperform large Spark clusters. He also discusses DuckDB's WebAssembly compilation for browser-based analytics, the 'never give up, never surrender' memory management project, and DuckDB Labs' bootstrapped business model of customer contracts rather than VC funding.
key_points: DuckDB invented the 'in-process OLAP' category — an embedded analytical database that runs as a library within your application, enabling zero-copy data interchange
The vectorized execution engine processes 1,024-value chunks to amortize interpretation overhead, keeping intermediates in CPU cache for better performance

Single-node execution has been neglected for 20 years in favor of distributed systems, even though most datasets easily fit on a laptop — a single DuckDB node matched 32 Spark nodes on the taxi dataset
DuckDB cross-compiles to WebAssembly with only ~30% performance loss, enabling browser-based OLAP that traditional heavyweight engines cannot achieve
DuckDB Labs is bootstrapped without VC money, monetizing through customer contracts for custom work and prioritization rather than giving away software while burning cash
money_quotes: DuckDB is a database as a library. We call it the in-process OLAP. And it's the first one because we kind of invented this as a class of systems, the in-process OLAP that just didn't exist before.

People for the last 20 or so years have been working really hard on building distributed execution engines. And in my opinion, people have been neglecting the single node execution performance a lot. As a matter of fact, they've been neglecting it so much that single node execution has actually become slower.

We ran a query on this taxi dataset, used DuckDB. And then we looked how big does the Spark cluster have to be to match this performance. And I think the answer was 32 or something like that. And that's a 32X increase in cost.
There's this misconception that everybody is sitting on these petabytes, but studies show that the majority of data sets people actually look at easily fits on something like a laptop.

We want to make sure that even if you're running out of memory, we still are able to complete the query successfully. So this is where we actively work on what we call the 'never give up, never surrender' project.
We haven't taken VC money. So we're not sitting on a giant pile of cash that we're slowly shoveling into a big fire.
We ran DuckDB on a Raspberry Pi and we ran SQLite on a $5,000 server running the same benchmark and DuckDB ended up being faster on the Raspberry Pi.

Traditional engines are far too heavyweight to be considered for cross-compilation games. You have hundreds of dependencies including like three compilers or something like that. And that's just not going to happen. You're not going to put that in the browser.
posts.key_insight: The in-process OLAP architecture eliminates the coordination overhead and data transfer bottlenecks of distributed systems, unlocking performance on single nodes that most people don't realize is possible.

posts.summary: Hannes Mühleisen and Mark Raasveldt present the State of the Duck at DuckCon #2, reviewing DuckDB's progress over the past ten months across three major releases (0.4, 0.5, 0.6). The talk covers significant improvements in storage compression, out-of-core query processing, parallelism, and new SQL features. Hannes discusses DuckDB Labs growing to 15 staff in an Amsterdam office, DuckDB reaching one million Python installs per month, and the governance structure with the non-profit DuckDB Foundation ensuring the MIT license. The roadmap focuses on storage format finalization for V1.0, remaining out-of-core operators, partition awareness, and Lakehouse format support.
key_points: DuckDB's storage format has been dramatically improved with lightweight specialized compression algorithms that approach Parquet file sizes while being faster to read, avoiding the performance cost of general-purpose compression like Zstandard

Out-of-core processing now covers sorting, hash joins, and window operators so queries gracefully spill to disk instead of failing with out-of-memory errors, with aggregation as the last remaining operator

DuckDB Labs is a self-funded bootstrapped company of ~15 staff, deliberately co-located in Amsterdam for faster collaboration, while the DuckDB Foundation as a non-profit ensures the project remains MIT-licensed independent of commercial interests
Adoption has grown dramatically: Python installs went from 150K to 1M per month, Java JDBC downloads jumped from 6K to 600K (indicating enterprise adoption), and DuckDB has no telemetry by design

Multi-database support and pluggable catalogs are coming, allowing DuckDB to attach and write to multiple databases including SQLite, making DuckDB's own storage format a first-class citizen alongside Parquet
money_quotes: One of our goals is to basically be able to squeeze as much out of a single machine, out of the laptop as possible. So that you can always get your queries running without having to resort to any sort of like distributed system setup that's very complex to set up, very complex to run.

It's probably going to happen that you will not see changes for a long time, and at some point we'll just miraculously declare it stable because we haven't changed it in so long that we're comfortable.
We literally do not know who is using DuckDB. And there are some frontends like the WebAssembly one... we see one download and there might be thousands of people using that one thing.

We actually are together in our office and I think it's part of the reason why we're able to move so quickly, because we can just yell at each other over coffee if we have a disagreement instead of writing passive aggressive emails.
It is in the statutes of the foundation that DuckDB has to remain under the MIT license. That is one of the things that we wanted to make absolutely sure that no matter what happens, this is insured.
Now is the time where I can complain that I really don't like the term Lakehouse. But I think the data formats that the Lakehouse people have generated, they're here to stay.
The entire database lives in that JDBC driver, so you don't actually connect to something, you run DuckDB inside your JVM, which is wild.
It doesn't have to bow to our commercial goals. So it's really an important thing especially in the context of this very common conflict in open source projects run by people working in a company.
posts.key_insight: DuckDB's strategy of maximizing single-machine performance through lightweight compression, out-of-core processing, and pervasive parallelism aims to eliminate the need for complex distributed systems, while its non-profit foundation governance ensures the open-source project remains independent of commercial pressures.

posts.summary: Hannes Mühleisen recounts the origin story of DuckDB, which grew out of research at CWI in the Netherlands. After engaging with R community data practitioners, he discovered they avoided databases entirely despite working with large datasets, due to slow client protocols and the burden of managing database servers. A prototype called MonoDB Lite proved the concept of in-process analytical databases but hit architectural limits, leading Hannes and Mark Raasveldt to build DuckDB from scratch—a multi-year effort that created an entirely new class of database system.
key_points: R community practitioners avoided databases entirely, preferring CSV files and hand-rolled data frame engines, because client-server setup was too burdensome
Database client protocols built in the 1980s were a major bottleneck—row-based, heavy serialization, never modernized
Running in-process is a harsher environment than client-server: you can't crash, can't modify global state like locale or working directory
The MonoDB Lite prototype validated product-market fit before DuckDB was built from scratch, reducing risk of the rewrite
Academic impact in data systems comes from building widely-used systems, not writing papers—citing Stonebraker's Turing Award as evidence
money_quotes: It turns out that they weren't using databases at all, right? They were storing their data in CSV files.
The client protocols of databases, built in the eighties, have never been touched, row-based, hardcore serialization stuff, not great.
Maybe we need to do something like SQLite, where there is actually no database server, but the database is in process. It's a library. But for analytics.
It turns out we invented a whole new class of database systems.
If you're running in-process, it's a much more harsh environment. You cannot just exit the process if things go wrong, because you're running in somebody else's process.
I decided that I'm going to start with Mark together to build a new database system, which is a monumental undertaking. It doesn't happen very often, and it's not successful 99% of the time.
My definition of success is not to write papers, it's to have impact.
We have a Turing Award in computer science for Mike Stonebraker, not because he writes amazing theoretical papers, but because he made systems like Ingress and Postgres.
posts.key_insight: DuckDB was born from discovering that data practitioners refused to use databases due to client-server complexity, revealing an entirely missing category: the in-process analytical database.

posts.summary: In this podcast interview, Hannes Mühleisen and Jordan Tigani discuss the origin of DuckDB and how MotherDuck was formed to commercialize it. Hannes explains how DuckDB emerged from academic research at CWI when he and Mark Raasveldt noticed state-of-the-art database ideas weren't reaching practitioners, largely due to deployment friction and dependency management. Jordan describes discovering DuckDB through benchmarks and recognizing that most data workloads don't require distributed systems. The conversation covers their rapid partnership formation, the scale-up vs scale-out debate, and the deliberate choice to keep DuckDB open while building a complementary commercial cloud product.
key_points: DuckDB's zero-dependency design was born from Hannes's frustration with restrictive academic IT environments where installing software was nearly impossible
VCs wanted DuckDB to become a Snowflake-style company, but the team chose to be technology providers instead, keeping the project open and flexible

Jordan's validation of the scale-up approach was pivotal — someone with BigQuery and SingleStore credentials taking a single-node system seriously countered years of ridicule from distributed systems advocates
The insight that most data isn't actually big was initially just a feeling for the DuckDB team until Google published data showing 95th percentile ML job sizes were around 100 gigabytes
DuckDB's competitive advantage comes from focusing on the complete end-to-end experience rather than just query execution speed
money_quotes: We noticed great ideas existed but weren't having real-world impact. People weren't using state-of-the-art solutions.
It seemed insane—two people writing a database management system when these typically require hundreds of people over ten years. But I tend to leap without looking sometimes.
I developed an uncanny ability to run things without Docker and a deep hatred for dependencies. That conviction shaped DuckDB's design: it can't have dependencies.
VCs wanted us to become a Snowflake, but we didn't want that. We wanted to be technology providers, staying open and flexible.
We'd been ridiculed for not taking distributed systems seriously, yet someone with his credentials validated our approach. That was shocking.
This transformation from scale-out to scale-up was surprising because our scale-up idea was based on feeling. We disliked systems like Spark based on intuition. We lacked data.
It is really shocking to see what is possible on laptops. It's something that we have kind of forgotten about.
There's also a big difference between what the cycle costs and what you are paying for the cycle.
posts.key_insight: DuckDB's success came from rejecting the distributed systems orthodoxy, eliminating deployment friction through zero dependencies, and focusing on the complete end-to-end user experience rather than raw query performance.

It is really shocking to see what is possible on laptops. It's something that we have kind of forgotten about.

posts.summary: Hannes Mühleisen presents the State of the Duck at DuckCon #3, covering DuckDB's progress over the past four months across versions 0.7 and 0.8. Key additions include multi-database attach support with pluggable storage backends (including SQLite), parallel JSON reading with auto-detection, hive-partition write support, pivot/unpivot operations, and as-of joins. He previews version 0.9 features including out-of-core hash aggregation with graceful degradation, compressed materialization to reduce memory footprint, Apache Iceberg read support, and improved cloud platform integration. Adoption metrics show 1.3 million Python installations per month and the project recently hit 10K GitHub stars.
key_points: DuckDB's pluggable database architecture allows attaching multiple storage backends including SQLite, enabling full read/write transactional access to SQLite databases using DuckDB's SQL dialect
Out-of-core hash aggregation in v0.9 uses radix-partitioned hash tables to enable graceful degradation when memory is exceeded, spilling to disk incrementally rather than failing
Compressed materialization reduces memory footprint during processing by applying frame-of-reference compression and converting small strings to integers, at essentially no runtime cost
DuckDB deliberately maintains row order unlike standard SQL, enabling features like positional joins that are useful for data frame-style workloads and ML pipelines
Iceberg support is positioned as complementary to DuckDB's native storage rather than a replacement, since Iceberg's cloud-native overhead is unnecessary for local compute
money_quotes: Something that we always try to do is try to make things easy to use so that while you don't bother us, you don't ask any questions to us.
Because people are wrangling quite large datasets with DuckDB on often very small machines, I mean, that's also what we tell you to do. So that's not your fault, of course.
You get this super graceful degradation so that even if your hash table, if it's, say, 80% of the hash table fits in memory, you won't end up with a big performance penalty.
Instead of having these pointers to the strings that end up consuming around 16 bytes per string, you can compress them and actually turn strings into integers, which is kind of cool.

Iceberg is super cool, of course, but we're not switching away from the DuckDB storage to Iceberg. If you are running on your local computer, Iceberg introduces a lot of overheads that you may not really want to deal with.
SQL doesn't have a concept of order of rows, but DuckDB does try quite hard to maintain the order as specified by the user.

Something that's actually kind of funny that we discovered later is this GDAL library also allows you to read Excel files. So through the spatial extension, you can now read Excel files. That was not intentional.
posts.key_insight: DuckDB's architecture enables graceful handling of datasets exceeding memory through radix-partitioned hash tables and compressed materialization, allowing powerful analytics on small machines without catastrophic failure.

posts.summary: Hannes Mühleisen and Mark Raasveldt present the State of the Duck at DuckCon #4, covering DuckDB's explosive growth (5 million extension installs per month, 2TB/day of downloads, half a million unique web visitors) and the roadmap toward version 1.0. The release strategy involves a 'Snow Duck' approach inspired by Apple's Snow Leopard—no new features, just stability and robustness. A major milestone is the introduction of backwards-compatible database file format, positioning DuckDB as a potential de facto format for storing tabular data. New features in v0.10 include multi-database attach for Postgres/MySQL, a 3x faster CSV reader, fixed-length arrays, unified memory manager, secret manager, and ALP floating point compression. The talk also covers the team's lean operational philosophy, noting they lack VC funding and maintain a tiny DevRel team.

posts.summary: Hannes Mühleisen explains the technical foundations of DuckDB in this Developer Voices podcast, focusing on two key innovations: vectorized query execution and morsel-driven parallelism. He describes how DuckDB processes data in chunks of 2,048 rows to keep computations in CPU cache, achieving roughly 100x speedups over naive approaches. He contrasts this with exchange-based parallelism used by systems like Spark, which suffers from skew and inflexibility. Mühleisen also discusses DuckDB's origins as an academic project at CWI Amsterdam that prioritized usability and practical impact over theoretical novelty, arguing that most real-world data problems fit on a single machine and don't need distributed systems.

posts.summary: Hannes Mühleisen shares the story of DuckDB's creation at the CWI research institute in the Netherlands, drawing parallels to Python's origin at the same institution. He explains DuckDB's core philosophy of simplicity and in-process architecture, inspired by SQLite's success, and details how it bridges the gap between academic database research and practical usability. He candidly discusses the tensions of building production software in academia — the safe space for prototyping versus the career risks of not publishing papers — and how the team eventually had to leave CWI to form DuckDB Labs. He advocates for bootstrapping over venture capital funding, arguing that VC money inevitably leads to license changes or project bastardization, and explains how DuckDB Labs has been profitable from day one through support contracts.

The in-process architecture eliminates the data transfer bottleneck and management overhead of traditional client-server databases, enabling zero-copy integration with pandas and Arrow

posts.summary: Hannes Mühleisen traces data management from 5,000-year-old Sumerian clay tablets to modern cloud databases, arguing that tables, SQL, and ACID transactions form an eternal 'holy trifecta' that keeps winning despite repeated attempts to replace them. He walks through the NoSQL era where Google abandoned SQL for MapReduce due to hardware constraints, only to see SQL return within four years via Hive. MongoDB added schema validation in 2017, Cassandra added ACID in 2023, and Databricks brought back integrated storage. He predicts relational systems will absorb key-value stores, document databases, time series, vector databases, and graph databases since all can be trivially represented as tables. Finally, he argues 'big data is dead' because modern single-node hardware is so powerful that distributing computation often makes things slower, demonstrating DuckDB on one machine beating Spark on 32 distributed nodes.
key_points: Tables, SQL, and ACID form a 'holy trifecta' that has survived every attempt to replace them — NoSQL systems like MongoDB, Cassandra, and Databricks all eventually re-adopted the principles they abandoned
Every NoSQL innovation shifted complexity onto application developers (schema management, eventual consistency, storage management), which consistently proved to be a terrible idea

Relational systems will eat key-value stores, document databases, time series, vector databases, and graph databases because all can be represented as trivial tables — only array-shaped data like images and video truly resists
Modern single-node hardware (400 GB/s RAM throughput, 5.5 GB/s SSDs) has made distribution unnecessary for most workloads — DuckDB on one machine beats Spark on 32 nodes with 1,000+ CPU cores
Data management systems never die once entrenched — DB2, Oracle, and Postgres persist because civilizations build on top of them
money_quotes: Tables were there before our civilization, and they will be there after our civilization.
There's this holy trifecta: Tables, SQL, ACID. The alternative is to have app developers do this themselves. And having app developers do this themselves is really not a great idea.
The first tables that we have are about 1,000 years older than the first texts that we have. Record-keeping actually predates literature by about 1,000 years.

If the idea is to say, let's have a separate database because embeddings are so fundamentally different, I need 200 million in VC money to start a new database company... Well, it's just a table that has a list of fixed size as its value. And that's all there is to it.
If I were working at a vector database company, I would be a bit worried at this point.
Once you start distributing a problem, it becomes just so much harder that you're very often better off with a single computer.
Even if we add 32 computers to this problem, and over 1,000 CPU cores versus the 32 we started with, Spark will still be slower than DuckDB on a single machine.

Every year there's a new hype. There's 50 new data systems appear. They get absorbed into Oracle after five years. It's great for VCs, it's great for the founders, but it's not particularly interesting.
posts.key_insight: The relational model's holy trifecta of tables, SQL, and ACID transactions has outlasted every attempt to replace it, and modern single-node hardware has eliminated the primary justification for distributed systems that drove the NoSQL movement.

posts.summary: Hannes Mühleisen announces official DuckDB support for Delta Lake and the Unity Catalog at the Data + AI Summit 2024, shortly after the milestone DuckDB 1.0 release. He positions DuckDB as filling the gap between Excel and Spark for last-mile data analysis. The Delta Lake integration leverages the delta kernel built by Databricks and the community, avoiding the need to build from scratch. He also announces DuckDB community extensions, allowing anyone to publish and distribute DuckDB extensions easily.
key_points: DuckDB 1.0 was released after six years of R&D, establishing a stable SQL dialect, APIs, and backwards-compatible storage format
DuckDB fills the gap between Excel and Spark for last-mile data analysis on datasets too large for spreadsheets but too small for distributed computing
The Delta Lake integration uses the delta kernel from Databricks, delegating file-reading complexity rather than building from scratch
A Unity Catalog extension enables DuckDB to discover and query Delta Lake tables as if they were local tables
DuckDB community extensions launch allows anyone to publish extensions that users can install with a simple command
money_quotes: It was the culmination of six years of R&D in data management systems.
There's a lot of data sets that are not going to work in Excel, but they are maybe a bit too small to actually throw Spark at them.
DuckDB is really perfect for this last mile of data analysis, where you may not need a whole data center to compute something.
So how are we going to do that? Obviously, we're going to use the best tool for the job available, right? CSV files. Maybe not.
One thing that's really special about the Delta Lake integration is that we use this delta kernel that Databricks is building with the community.

We're really excited about Tabular and Databricks bringing Delta Lake and Iceberg closer together because for us, it means we don't have to maintain two different things for essentially the same problem.
From now on, actually, we are launching community extensions, which means that everyone can make DuckDB extensions and basically publish them.
posts.key_insight: DuckDB bridges the gap between spreadsheets and distributed computing for last-mile analysis, and its new Delta Lake integration via the shared delta kernel demonstrates how open ecosystem collaboration reduces duplicated effort for everyone.

posts.summary: Hannes Mühleisen discusses DuckDB's origins, design philosophy, and technical architecture on Software Engineering Daily. He explains how DuckDB drew inspiration from SQLite's ease of use while swapping in an analytical query engine designed for columnar workloads. The conversation covers why C++11 was chosen over C or newer languages, DuckDB's three main use cases (prototyping, local data analysis, enterprise pipeline components), and its ability to query directly on external file formats like Parquet and CSV without ingestion. Hannes describes the 'tail scale' philosophy of solving data problems for the 99% rather than the 1%, and explains DuckDB Labs' bootstrapped business model funded by consulting and support rather than VC money.
key_points: DuckDB takes SQLite's in-process simplicity and replaces the engine with a columnar analytical query processor, enabling queries on billions of rows locally
DuckDB queries external file formats (Parquet, CSV, Arrow) directly without ingestion, using statistics and column pruning for performance optimization
The 'tail scale' philosophy targets the 99% of data problems that fit on single machines, rather than the distributed computing problems that dominate academic research
DuckDB Labs is entirely bootstrapped through consulting and support contracts, with no VC funding, while the project itself is owned by a non-profit foundation under MIT license
A novel MVCC scheme was designed specifically for bulk columnar updates and large transactions, handling operations like ingesting two terabytes or adding computed columns without full table rewrites
money_quotes: We set out to solve the data problems for the 99% of us, because it's so common to sort of solve the problems of the one percenters.

People love solving Google's data problems in their research, in their work and systems because yeah, if it doesn't scale, it's useless. But we completely forget that 99% of data problems doesn't actually need any of that.
It's almost like one of these tank engines that can take any kind of fuel. We don't have to ingest data into our own storage from it before we can run queries.
Nothing teaches you SQL as much as writing your own data system, because you have to write the engine that interprets other people's crazy SQL queries.

I'm one of these people that kind of leaps and then starts looking. It's probably good that it's like this because I would have never done it otherwise. I have underestimated the effort to make a database management system greatly.
We have the world's best CSV reader because it's the first thing you do with your data system. And if that doesn't work, you're immediately frustrated.
I don't think there's a use case too small. And I think that's also what sets DuckDB apart from the competition.
We accidentally have built this execution engine for Turing complete language, which is terrifying.
posts.key_insight: By applying SQLite's in-process simplicity to analytical workloads and enabling direct queries on external file formats, DuckDB solves the data problems of the 99% who don't need distributed computing.

posts.summary: Hannes Mühleisen opens DuckCon #5 with a state-of-the-duck overview, describing DuckDB's mission to replace fear of data with confidence through simple, dependency-free tooling. He outlines three emerging use cases: interactive analysis, pipeline components, and creative architectures (mobile, browser, Lambda, satellites). Adoption metrics are staggering—6 million Python downloads/month (up from 2M in January), 17 million extension installs/month, and 600K unique web visitors. Mark Raasveldt then previews DuckDB 1.1, highlighting community extensions, dynamic SQL improvements, parallel streaming queries, and dynamic filter pushdown from joins. The team emphasizes that DuckDB Labs funds development through commercial support contracts rather than VC funding, and that post-1.0 focus is shifting toward extensions and the ecosystem edge rather than core bloat.
key_points: DuckDB's core mission is transforming data from something feared into something approachable—like opening a movie file in VLC rather than wrestling with hostile tooling

Three distinct use cases have emerged: interactive analysis (the original target), pipeline components (embedding DuckDB as a step in enterprise data pipelines), and creative architectures (mobile, browser, Lambda, satellites)
Adoption has tripled in months: 6M Python downloads/month, 17M extension installs/month, 200TB/month served via Cloudflare
Community extensions launch with a simple PR-based workflow, cross-platform CI/CD handled by the team, and Rust support coming soon via a stable C API
Post-1.0 development is deliberately shifting toward extensions and ecosystem edges to keep the core system lean and unbloated
money_quotes: People generally fear data. Data sets can be unruly. You're gonna have that wrong comma in line 400,000 or something like that.
The tools people generally are using to look at data, they tended to be, I think, hostile is the right word. Hard to use, hard to install, hard to set up, hard to make happy.
Data sets should just be harmless. It should be just like a movie file. You just throw it in a VLC and it's gonna be fine. Nobody's afraid of movie files anymore.
It's really the first custom-built in-process analytical data management system, which is kind of an accident. We didn't really set out to make a new category, but there we are.

You can't just install Postgres on an iPhone. You can't run Spark on an iPhone either. You can put DuckDB in a Lambda function. You can run DuckDB in a browser. You can run DuckDB on a satellite in space.
In the past you couldn't just stick a Spark cluster into a pipeline. That doesn't work. But with DuckDB you can just spin up an instance and run some minor transformation. That's perfectly fine.
DuckDB is not VC funded. The company that employs the core contributors funds the whole show by offering commercial support contracts and consulting contracts.
When we were pushing out 1.0, you have this anxiety—five minutes later somebody will be like, oh by the way, the joins are broken. Didn't happen.
posts.key_insight: DuckDB's explosive adoption stems from replacing hostile, complex data tooling with an embeddable system so simple that data becomes as approachable as opening a movie file.

posts.summary: Hannes Mühleisen presents DuckDB's potential to disrupt traditional two-tier and three-tier data architectures by embedding the query engine at every layer of the stack. He argues that DuckDB's in-process architecture eliminates the need for centralized database servers, enabling deployment in operational servers, app servers, Lambdas, browsers, and mobile devices. He demonstrates how companies like Fivetran, Oktar, and Hugging Face are already using DuckDB to replace heavyweight infrastructure like Spark clusters. The core thesis is that unshackling a state-of-the-art query engine from fixed servers represents a massive paradigm shift in data architecture.

DuckDB can replace Spark clusters in data pipelines by running in Lambdas with millisecond startup time, dramatically reducing cost and complexity
The in-process model enables zero-copy data exchange with Pandas and Arrow data frames—no sockets, no serialization, just reading memory directly

DuckDB can be everywhere in your stack, same SQL, same storage format, same capabilities. It's a state of the art processing engine that's now kind of unshackled from DB service.

posts.summary: Hannes Mühleisen presents DuckDB's ACID transaction capabilities as a solution to common data reliability problems in the Python data stack. DuckDB's in-process architecture enables zero-copy access to pandas DataFrames and Arrow structures, eliminating data transfer overhead
Transactions can be combined with integrity checks (like row count assertions) to create self-validating data pipelines that automatically roll back on failure

posts.summary: In this podcast interview, Hannes Mühleisen discusses DuckDB's origins at CWI, its growth to 10 million monthly downloads, and its design philosophy as a universal data wrangling tool. posts.summary: Hannes Mühleisen traces the origin story of DuckDB back to 2015, when analytical data management was fragmented between expensive enterprise systems, Hadoop, immature cloud offerings, and academic spinoffs that got swallowed by corporations. He and Mark Raasveldt decided to build 'the SQLite for analytics' — a single-node, embedded, vectorized, CPU-only, open-source database. posts.summary: Hannes Mühleisen and Mark Raasveldt introduce DuckLake, a new lakehouse format that replaces the complex file-based metadata layers of Iceberg and Delta with a standard database (like Postgres or DuckDB) for metadata storage while keeping Parquet files on blob storage for data. They argue that existing lakehouse formats like Iceberg were built on the Hadoop-era premise that databases don't scale, yet ironically ended up requiring a database (Postgres) as a catalog server anyway, without revisiting their original file-based design decisions. DuckLake's three principles are simplicity (just a database plus file storage), scalability (metadata is ~100,000x smaller than data, and the architecture mirrors proven designs from Snowflake and BigQuery), and speed (single round-trip queries instead of multiple file hops). The format enables features impossible in existing lakehouses: multi-table transactions, data inlining for small writes, zero-trust encryption via Parquet encryption with keys in the metadata store, and consistent catalog-level snapshots. DuckLake serves as DuckDB's answer to multiplayer/multi-user scenarios without implementing a traditional client-server protocol.

posts.summary: Hannes Mühleisen presents DuckLake, a new open table format that replaces the complex metadata layers of Iceberg (Avro files, JSON manifests, REST catalogs) with a simple SQL database for metadata storage while keeping Parquet files on blob storage. DuckLake stores all metadata in any SQL database (Postgres, DuckDB, etc.) and data files on any blob storage, enabling cross-table commits, efficient small writes, per-file encryption, and virtually unlimited snapshots. He demonstrates the system working with both local DuckDB files and remote Postgres+S3 configurations, showing time travel, deletes, updates, and schema changes. The format reuses Iceberg's Parquet-based delete file format and plans zero-copy import/export with Iceberg for interoperability.

posts.summary: Hannes Mühleisen presents DuckLake, a new lakehouse format that replaces Iceberg's complex file-based metadata architecture with a simple SQL database for catalog management. He traces the evolution from single-node databases through shared-nothing architectures to separated storage and compute, showing how Iceberg ironically reintroduced the database it tried to eliminate via its REST catalog. DuckLake acknowledges this reality by using a proper database for metadata alongside object storage for data files, eliminating layers of Avro and JSON metadata files. The result is dramatically simpler setup, fewer round trips, and 4x better performance than Iceberg in benchmarks.
key_points: Iceberg's architecture evolved to require a database catalog anyway, but retained its complex file-based metadata layers, creating unnecessary complexity
DuckLake replaces the entire Iceberg metadata stack (JSON, Avro, manifest lists) with simple SQL tables in any transactional database
The three scaling dimensions—storage, compute, and metadata—can be independently chosen and scaled from laptop to global deployment
DuckLake achieves 4x faster performance than Iceberg by reducing query planning from 5 sequential round trips to just one database query plus data file reads
Features like row inlining, per-file encryption, multi-table transactions, and Iceberg import/export come naturally from having a real database for metadata
money_quotes: You just told us you're doing all this stuff to not have a database. And now you're adding the database back, but you never go back to your original design decisions about not having a database and having all these files.

I will never understand the decision of having four different encodings for data in one file format. You have JSON, you have Avro, and you have Parquet, and you have the whole REST thing for the catalog. It's kind of crazy.
The biggest reaction we got was like, oh, it's so obvious. And we're like, yes, but you didn't do it.
It takes a PhD in computer science and a day of screaming to get this done. This is like 10 seconds.
Yesterday, somebody told me that databases are considered legacy. And I'm like, yeah, it's a 5,000 year cultural technology. It's not legacy.
If all you have is files, then making everything be a file is kind of logical.
Cutting out things is making them better. Having fewer dependencies, having fewer things that can go wrong, having fewer random Avro problems impacting the performance of your data storage.
I actually managed to crash the JVM by running the official Iceberg Spark plugin on my laptop. I thought, how on earth has nobody tried this? This is the first thing you try.
posts.key_insight: The lakehouse world spent years trying to eliminate databases from metadata management only to add them back via catalogs, proving that a simple SQL database for metadata plus object storage for data was always the right architecture.

posts.summary: Hannes Mühleisen argues that databases and their core abstractions—tables, SQL, and ACID—are fundamentally worthy of enthusiasm and affection, despite widespread criticism. He traces table technology back 5,000 years to Mesopotamian clay tablets, predating written text by a millennium, establishing tables as a foundational civilizational technique. He recounts how DuckDB was born from frustration with Spark's poor developer experience, aiming to make large dataset processing work simply on a laptop. He critiques MongoDB for abandoning these proven abstractions, noting that developers end up reimplementing schema validation, joins, and consistency checks by hand—and that MongoDB eventually added all three features back. He demonstrates DuckDB's vectorized execution achieving near-theoretical hardware limits, and concludes that the elegance of database abstractions should be judged separately from the clunkiness of legacy implementations.
key_points: Tables predate written text by 1,000 years—they are a 5,000-year-old civilizational technique, not just a software concept
The 'holy trinity' of databases—tables, SQL, and ACID—provides abstractions so fundamental that abandoning them forces developers to reimplement them poorly in application code
DuckDB was born from the realization that Spark's poor developer experience came from ignoring everything beyond core algorithms—the 'bun around the hamburger meat'
Vectorized execution processes batches of 1,000 rows at a time instead of row-at-a-time, achieving near-theoretical CPU limits (3.7 billion operations/sec on a 4 GHz CPU)
Criticisms of databases conflate old implementations (Oracle, DB2) with the underlying abstractions, which remain elegant and powerful
money_quotes: We had tables 1,000 years before we had text. So this is a fundamental civilization technique that's older than the written text.
There is a schema, it's just you don't know what it is.
If you don't have tables, SQL and ACID, your developers will have to implement them by hand.

If you don't have ACID, then it means that your developers get to deal with consistency problems in their application. And your web developers probably are not the people that you want to be implementing consistency checks.
Tables, SQL and ACID do spark joy, and MongoDB does not.
The abstraction isn't affected by the shittiness of the implementations.
I think databases are the most complex pieces of software out there. You need kind of all of computer science to do it.
Just because something is new and shiny doesn't mean it's better from an abstraction perspective.
posts.key_insight: The core database abstractions of tables, SQL, and ACID are 5,000-year-old civilizational techniques that remain fundamentally sound—criticisms of databases are really criticisms of legacy implementations, not the abstractions themselves.

posts.summary: Hannes Mühleisen and Mark Raasveldt discuss DuckLake, their reimagination of lakehouse formats that replaces file-based metadata (JSON, Avro) with a SQL relational database for metadata management while keeping data files on object stores. They argue this dramatically simplifies the lakehouse stack, reducing round trips from six or seven to two, and enabling features like data inlining for small writes, per-file encryption, and cheap snapshots. DuckLake scales from a single local DuckDB file up to thousands of compute nodes, and can serve as a backend for the Iceberg REST catalog. The project leverages DuckDB's existing infrastructure and has received overwhelmingly positive community response despite being only at version 0.2.
key_points: DuckLake replaces file-based metadata (JSON, Avro files) with a SQL database, cutting query round trips from ~7 to 2 and eliminating significant architectural complexity
Data inlining allows small writes to go directly into the metadata store, making them immediately visible without buffering delays or tiny file proliferation
Per-file encryption with keys stored in the metadata catalog enables secure storage on completely untrusted object stores with a single boolean flag
Snapshots in DuckLake are just database rows rather than expensive file operations, enabling thousands of snapshots without performance degradation
DuckLake as a concept is engine-agnostic — Spark, Trino, and other engines can use DuckDB as a lightweight gateway to DuckLake metadata
money_quotes: We just looked at that stack at some point, because we are obviously the people that have to implement this in the end, because people kept asking us to make support for these things in DuckDB.

You switch on encryption and we will generate a unique encryption key for every Parquet file we write out to object storage and actually store the key in the metadata catalog. And so what you now have is you can put your Duck Lake on a completely untrusted storage and whoever has access to it can do absolutely nothing with those files.
Your client or your local machine is no longer sort of the smallest light in the architecture. But it's suddenly actually the prominent player because it's where the compute happens.
Once we accept that storage and compute are disconnected, what gravity is really left?
I think there's still such a huge gap between what is kind of possible and what is the daily reality of the data practitioners.
It's almost like there's half of the population has to drive a car from the 1920s. That would not be considered great. Let's maybe get a 1980s car. That would be a big improvement.
What surprised me the most was that it wasn't that hard. At every step of the way, we didn't really face any big hurdles. It all just kind of worked.
If the ecosystem maybe admittedly or unadmittedly adopt some of these ideas, we also consider this a huge success. Whether that's our ducky logo on it or not isn't such a huge thing.
posts.key_insight: Replacing file-based lakehouse metadata with a SQL database eliminates enormous complexity while preserving all the benefits of open table formats, and DuckDB's lightweight embeddability makes it a natural gateway for any engine to interact with this simpler architecture.

posts.summary: Hannes Mühleisen traces the evolution of data architecture from the 1985 three-tier model through cloud data warehouses to a proposed inverted architecture where compute happens on client devices. He argues that the traditional top-down pyramid—where a central data warehouse holds all power—has barely changed despite moving to the cloud, and that modern single-node query engines like DuckDB combined with lakehouse formats can flip this model. He demonstrates DuckDB processing 6 billion rows on a laptop with only 2GB of memory, and shows examples of running analytics on 13-year-old laptops, phones, satellites, and cars. The core proposal is that clients should be empowered with local compute while sharing data through commodity storage and open metadata formats.
key_points: The traditional three-tier data architecture from 1985 barely changed when moving to the cloud—it just became someone else's expensive server
Database client-server protocols are so slow at transferring data that simply copying a CSV file is often faster, possibly by strategic design to create vendor lock-in
Single-threaded implementations on modern hardware can beat distributed systems for the vast majority of real workloads, where the median query scans only 100MB and P99.9 uses under 300GB
The proposed inverted architecture puts compute on client devices and treats storage/metadata as commodity infrastructure, meaning more users equals more compute rather than more cloud costs
Lakehouse formats like Iceberg and DuckLake provide the coordination layer needed to prevent the chaos of everyone having their own disconnected data files

If you have more users, you have more compute. It's insane, right? You're running stuff on local devices.

These things are criminally incompetent at transferring data. And it might actually be strategic incompetence. Because if you're keeping the data hostage, then you can keep charging them for your superyacht purposes.
A halfway competent, single-threaded implementation can beat most distributed systems.

The ingredients are competent single-node query engines and Lakehouse formats to basically glue them all together.
posts.key_insight: The data architecture should be flipped upside down: instead of powerful central servers serving dumb clients, empowered client devices with local query engines should do the compute while sharing data through commodity storage and open lakehouse formats.
